title: Git Intro
content:
Git Introduction

Version Control System

Git is a popular distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 and maintained by Junio Hamano. It helps developers track changes in their code, collaborate with others, and manage project history.



Git lets you save snapshots of your work, revert to earlier versions, and work on different features simultaneously.

title: Where Git Is Used
content:
Where Git Is Used

Local and Remote Repositories

Git is used locally on your computer to manage versions of your code and with remote hosting services like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket to share and collaborate with others.



Using Git with remote repositories lets teams work together and keeps everyone’s changes in sync.

title: Installing Git
content:
Installing Git

Setup and Verification

Before using Git, you need to install it on your system. You can download the installer from the official Git site and follow the setup instructions. After installation, verify Git by running the following command in your terminal or command prompt:

Terminal
git --version

This sets up the core Git tools you will use for version control.

title: Git Configuration
content:
Git Configuration

Setting Your Identity

After installation, Git needs your identity to track commits. You configure this using the following global commands:

Terminal
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@example.com"

This tells Git exactly who made each change when you commit.

title: Getting Started
content:
Git Getting Started

Initializing a Repository

To start a Git repository in a folder, navigate to the folder in your terminal and run the initialize command:

Terminal
git init

This creates a hidden .git folder where Git securely tracks your changes and history. You now have a local repository that can track your project’s progress!

title: New Files
content:
Git New Files

Untracked Files

A new file in a Git repository is anything you’ve created but Git isn’t yet tracking. Use git status to see untracked files. Git doesn’t automatically track new files — you must add them to the staging area before they can be committed.

title: Staging
content:
Git Staging Environment

Preparing Changes

The staging area is where you prepare changes before committing them to the repository.



Terminal
git add <file>
git add --all

Use git add <file> to stage a file. You can stage all changes with git add --all or git add -A. Use git status to check staged files.

If you stage a file by mistake, use git restore --staged <file> to unstage it.

title: Commit
content:
Git Commit

Saving Snapshots

After staging changes, you commit them to the local repository with git commit -m "message". A commit saves a snapshot of the project’s staged changes along with a message describing them.

Terminal
git commit -m "Initial commit"
git commit -a -m "Update files"

You can also skip staging for tracked files by using git commit -a -m "message", which stages and commits modified files in one step.

title: Help
content:
Git Help

Built-in Documentation

Git provides a built-in help system you can use when you’re unsure about a command or its options. Use git help <command> or git <command> --help to view the manual page.

Terminal
git help commit

The command git help -g lists guides and concepts you can explore.

title: Push to Remote
content:
Git Push to Remote

Sharing Your Code

After you’ve committed your changes locally, you can send them to a remote repository (like GitHub, GitLab or Bitbucket) using git push.



Terminal
git push origin main

For example, git push origin pushes the current branch to the default remote named origin. Pushing allows others to see your changes and collaborate on the project.

title: Revert
content:
Git Revert

Undoing Safely

The git revert command undoes a previous commit by creating a new commit that reverses the changes. This keeps your commit history intact while undoing the effects of a specific commit.



Terminal
git revert HEAD

For example, the command above will create a new commit that undoes the latest commit. Reverting is a safe way to undo changes without altering the original commit history.

title: Reset
content:
Git Reset

Moving the HEAD

The git reset command moves the current branch (HEAD) to a different commit. Depending on the option, it can also change which changes are staged or even discard changes from your working directory.



Terminal
git reset --soft <commit>
git reset --mixed <commit>
git reset --hard <commit>
  • --soft: moves HEAD, keeps changes staged
  • --mixed: (default) unstages changes
  • --hard: discards changes entirely

This command is useful for cleaning up your history, but be careful with hard resets as they can permanently remove work.

title: Reflog
content:
Git Reflog

Recovering Lost Commits

git reflog records changes to the tip of branches and the HEAD reference. It lets you see where HEAD and branches have been — including changes you made by mistake — making it possible to recover lost commits or undo a reset.



Terminal
git reflog
git reset --hard HEAD@{1}

For example, after checking the reflog, you can identify a previous state and then use the reset command to return safely to it.

title: Amend
content:
Git Amend

Modifying the Last Commit

The git commit --amend command lets you modify the most recent commit. You can use it to fix typos in the commit message, add forgotten files, or remove files from the last commit.



Terminal
git add <forgotten_file>
git commit --amend

By staging a file and running amend, you will insert the newly staged changes directly into the previous commit instead of creating an entirely new one.

title: Recovery
content:
Git Recovery

Restoring Lost Work

Git recovery refers to the process of retrieving lost commits, branches, or changes you might have accidentally lost. Tools like git reflog help you see where HEAD and branches have been in the past, making it possible to restore lost states.

This is helpful when you undo changes or reset commits by mistake.

title: Fixing Mistakes
content:
Git Undo & Fixing Mistakes

Choosing the Right Tool

In Git, there are several ways to undo or fix mistakes:

  • git revert — creates a new commit that reverses changes from a previous commit. This keeps the history intact.
  • git reset — moves HEAD and can unstage changes or remove commits depending on the mode.
  • git reflog — shows a log of where HEAD has pointed, useful for recovery.

Choose the right command based on whether you want to preserve history or completely reset to a former state.

title: .gitignore
content:
Git .gitignore

Ignoring Files

The .gitignore file tells Git which files or directories to ignore. Files listed here will not be tracked or staged by Git.



Use patterns in the file to efficiently ignore build artifacts, secrets, or local configuration files. This helps keep repositories clean and free of unnecessary files.

title: Git LFS
content:
Git Large File Storage (LFS)

Handling Massive Files

Git LFS stands for Large File Storage. It helps manage large files (such as audio, video, datasets) by storing them outside the regular Git history and replacing them with lightweight text pointers.



This keeps repository size manageable and speeds up cloning operations. Git LFS is incredibly useful in projects that include large binaries or media files.

title: Final Summary
content:
Git Final Summary 🚀

Course Completed! 🏆

This concludes the Git tutorial. Git is a powerful distributed version control system used to track and manage changes in source code during software development. It allows developers to save snapshots of work, revert to earlier versions, collaborate with others, and maintain a history of a project’s evolution.

Throughout this tutorial, you learned how to:

  • Install and configure Git with your identity.
  • Initialize a Git repository and understand the Git lifecycle (working directory, staging area, local repository, and remote).
  • Track changes by staging and committing files with meaningful messages.
  • Work with branches to create isolated development paths and merge changes safely.
  • Interact with remote repositories — push and pull changes with platforms like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket.
  • Undo or recover work using commands like git revert, git reset, git reflog, and amend commits when necessary.
  • Manage repository settings such as .gitignore and Git LFS for handling large files.
  • Collaborate efficiently and maintain best practices for version control workflows.

With these skills, you are now equipped to use Git confidently for version control and collaboration in personal and professional projects!
